rationale: Neither candidate recovers GT's two actual concerns (stale docstring wording, confirming a live producer for the legacy `audio` key) with any precision, but Y's finding that the containment fallback is unconditional and can accept a wrong-but-longer transcript (e.g. 'hello' matching 'hello world goodbye') is thematically close to GT's point that the new fallback silently changes behavior for all callers, and Y also correctly narrates the core bug (postprocess writes `codes.audio`, preprocess previously read only legacy `audio`) matching the GT reviewer's own framing almost exactly. X's findings (dead code guard, hardcoded thresholds, docstring completeness nits) are all valid and grounded but mostly cosmetic/nit-level and never touch the core fix's correctness narrative. X is somewhat more actionable with concrete prescribed fixes, but Y's findings are more substantive and better grounded in cross-file verification.
